APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it targets dialogue systems and knowledge graph embeddings, introduces a new symmetric collaborative dialogue setting, and provides a substantial 11K-dialogue human dataset (MutualFriends). The paper proposes a novel neural model (DynoNet) combining evolving KG node embeddings with RNN generation, compares against rule-based and neural baselines, and uses appropriate automatic and human evaluations (task success and multiple human-rated metrics). Methodology, dataset scale, and evaluation style match expectations for a major NLP/ML conference, and the related work coverage and writing quality are reported as adequate. | B(5): This work addresses a core NLP/ML topic — goal-oriented dialogue with structured knowledge — introducing a new symmetric collaborative setting and an 11K human-human MutualFriends dataset, which is directly relevant to top-tier venues. The proposed DynoNet model (dynamic KG embeddings, message passing, entity abstraction) and the use of RNN encoder-decoder with attention/copy are methods expected at these conferences. Evaluation includes both automatic task success and human judgments (fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness) and comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines, matching community standards. Overall the paper is well positioned for a top NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it targets dialogue systems and knowledge graph embeddings, introduces a new symmetric collaborative dialogue setting, and provides a substantial 11K-dialogue human dataset (MutualFriends). The paper proposes a novel neural model (DynoNet) combining evolving KG node embeddings with RNN generation, compares against rule-based and neural baselines, and uses appropriate automatic and human evaluations (task success and multiple human-rated metrics). Methodology, dataset scale, and evaluation style match expectations for a major NLP/ML conference, and the related work coverage and writing quality are reported as adequate.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses a core NLP/ML topic — goal-oriented dialogue with structured knowledge — introducing a new symmetric collaborative setting and an 11K human-human MutualFriends dataset, which is directly relevant to top-tier venues. Evaluation includes both automatic task success and human judgments (fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness) and comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines, matching community standards. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a new symmetric collaborative dialogue formulation (MutualFriends) backed by a substantial 11K human-human dataset, which appears to be a novel problem framing and resource. The proposed DynoNet model—embedding an evolving knowledge graph updated from utterances with entity abstraction and message passing and conditioning generation on node embeddings—is a non-trivial technical extension beyond standard encoder–decoder or static KG approaches. The summary indicates the authors clearly differentiate their contributions from prior work and present empirical evidence of improvements, so the contribution is notably novel though not a radical departure from existing neural dialogue/KG techniques. | B(4): The paper introduces a distinct symmetric collaborative dialogue formulation and provides a new 11K human-human MutualFriends dataset, which together constitute a novel problem setup. The proposed DynoNet—an evolving knowledge-graph embedding with entity abstraction and node message passing integrated into an RNN generation model—appears to be a non-trivial extension beyond standard encoder-decoder and static KG approaches. The authors explicitly differentiate their setting, dataset, and model from prior rule-based and neural baselines, so the contribution would be informative to experts though not a radical departure from existing neural-KG and dialogue techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a new symmetric collaborative dialogue formulation (MutualFriends) backed by a substantial 11K human-human dataset, which appears to be a novel problem framing and resource. The proposed DynoNet model—embedding an evolving knowledge graph updated from utterances with entity abstraction and message passing and conditioning generation on node embeddings—is a non-trivial technical extension beyond standard encoder–decoder or static KG approaches. The summary indicates the authors clearly differentiate their contributions from prior work and present empirical evidence of improvements, so the contribution is notably novel though not a radical departure from existing neural dialogue/KG techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a distinct symmetric collaborative dialogue formulation and provides a new 11K human-human MutualFriends dataset, which together constitute a novel problem setup. The proposed DynoNet—an evolving knowledge-graph embedding with entity abstraction and node message passing integrated into an RNN generation model—appears to be a non-trivial extension beyond standard encoder-decoder and static KG approaches. The authors explicitly differentiate their setting, dataset, and model from prior rule-based and neural baselines, so the contribution would be informative to experts though not a radical departure from existing neural-KG and dialogue techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by experiments: the authors introduce the symmetric MutualFriends setting and collect an 11K-dialogue dataset, propose DynoNet (evolving KG embeddings with message passing and entity abstraction), and compare to rule-based and neural baselines using task-success and human evaluations. Mathematical development appears to be engineering/model design rather than formal proofs, and hyperparameters and training/dev losses are reported. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and no limitations are discussed, so there are minor gaps in experimental transparency and reproducibility that do not undermine the core technical contributions. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: a new MutualFriends dataset of ~11K dialogues, the DynoNet model (dynamic KG embeddings with message passing and entity abstraction), and evaluations showing improved task success and human-like behavior against rule-based and neural baselines. There are no mathematical proofs to verify, and the model design is plausibly described; experimental methodology includes reasonable baselines and human evaluations, although the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, detailed ablations, or code release. These are minor issues that do not undermine the core experimental support, so the work is technically sound overall.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are supported by experiments: the authors introduce the symmetric MutualFriends setting and collect an 11K-dialogue dataset, propose DynoNet (evolving KG embeddings with message passing and entity abstraction), and compare to rule-based and neural baselines using task-success and human evaluations. Mathematical development appears to be engineering/model design rather than formal proofs, and hyperparameters and training/dev losses are reported. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and no limitations are discussed, so there are minor gaps in experimental transparency and reproducibility that do not undermine the core technical contributions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: a new MutualFriends dataset of ~11K dialogues, the DynoNet model (dynamic KG embeddings with message passing and entity abstraction), and evaluations showing improved task success and human-like behavior against rule-based and neural baselines. There are no mathematical proofs to verify, and the model design is plausibly described; experimental methodology includes reasonable baselines and human evaluations, although the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, detailed ablations, or code release. These are minor issues that do not undermine the core experimental support, so the work is technically sound overall.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its DynoNet model against both rule-based and neural baselines on the same MutualFriends dataset using shared metrics (task success, human evaluations, and training/dev loss), and reports hyperparameters and adequate related-work coverage. This indicates fair and appropriate experimental comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate explicit discussion when prior methods perform comparably or better, nor confirmation that the very latest SOTA baselines were included, representing minor omissions. Overall the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares the proposed model to rule-based and neural baselines on the same MutualFriends dataset and uses appropriate metrics (task success, human ratings, training/dev loss), and related work coverage is reported as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate whether the most recent competing neural approaches or SOTA baselines were included, nor that the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better or provide in-depth analysis of the differences. Overall the comparisons appear good but with minor omissions in baselines and analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its DynoNet model against both rule-based and neural baselines on the same MutualFriends dataset using shared metrics (task success, human evaluations, and training/dev loss), and reports hyperparameters and adequate related-work coverage. reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares the proposed model to rule-based and neural baselines on the same MutualFriends dataset and uses appropriate metrics (task success, human ratings, training/dev loss), and related work coverage is reported as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate whether the most recent competing neural approaches or SOTA baselines were included, nor that the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better or provide in-depth analysis of the differences. Overall the comparisons appear good but with minor omissions in baselines and analysis.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem by formalizing a symmetric collaborative dialogue setting and providing a substantial 11K human-human MutualFriends dataset, which fills a gap in grounded multi-agent dialogue data. The proposed DynoNet approach—dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings with message passing and entity abstraction—offers a concrete technical advance that demonstrably improves task success and human-likeness over rule-based and neural baselines. These contributions are likely to influence how researchers model evolving structured knowledge in dialogue and to open new directions for grounded collaborative agents, with applicability beyond this specific task. | B(4): The paper tackles an important and underexplored problem—symmetric collaborative dialogue with private knowledge—by introducing a new 11K-dialogue MutualFriends dataset and a model (DynoNet) that integrates evolving knowledge-graph embeddings with language generation. Demonstrated improvements in task success and human-likeness over rule-based and neural baselines indicate the approach meaningfully advances how structured knowledge and dialogue interact. This combination of a realistic dataset and a novel modeling technique is likely to influence further research on collaborative dialogue and KG-informed generation, though it is not clearly transformative across the entire NLP field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem by formalizing a symmetric collaborative dialogue setting and providing a substantial 11K human-human MutualFriends dataset, which fills a gap in grounded multi-agent dialogue data. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a solid set of experiments: an 11K human-human dataset, comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines, automatic and human evaluations (task success, fluency/correctness/cooperation/human-likeness), and training/dev loss reporting, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the DynoNet components, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but reproducibility is uncertain because code availability is not mentioned despite hyperparameter reporting. Overall the empirical evidence is adequate but missing important analyses and explicit discussion of limitations. | B(3): The experiments include a new 11K-dialogue dataset, comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines, automatic task-success metrics, and human evaluations (fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness), which supports the core claims. However, the summary does not mention any ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contributions of entity abstraction or message-passing, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and hyperparameters are provided, but code availability is not mentioned, leaving some reproducibility questions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a solid set of experiments: an 11K human-human dataset, comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines, automatic and human evaluations (task success, fluency/correctness/cooperation/human-likeness), and training/dev loss reporting, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the DynoNet components, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but reproducibility is uncertain because code availability is not mentioned despite hyperparameter reporting. Overall the empirical evidence is adequate but missing important analyses and explicit discussion of limitations.

However, the summary does not mention any ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contributions of entity abstraction or message-passing, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the methodology is described clearly (evolving knowledge graph, node embeddings, RNN encoder–decoder with copying) and the MutualFriends dataset and evaluation setup are presented. Figures/tables and examples appear to effectively support the experimental claims (automatic and human evaluations) and hyperparameters are reported. Minor presentation gaps include a lack of stated limitations and no mention of released code, which slightly reduces completeness but not overall clarity. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from problem setup through the DynoNet method to experiments on the 11K MutualFriends dialogues and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early (symmetric setting, dataset, DynoNet). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined (message passing, entity abstraction, RNN with attention and copy), and experiments/tables (task success, human ratings) effectively communicate results. Minor presentation issues include the lack of an explicit limitations section and no mention of code availability, which would improve reproducibility and clarity.

main_claims: Introduce a symmetric collaborative dialogue setting where two agents with private knowledge bases must communicate to identify a shared item.
Collected a dataset of 11K human-human dialogues (MutualFriends setting) exhibiting diverse lexical, semantic, and strategic phenomena.
Propose a neural model (DynoNet) that embeds an evolving knowledge graph with entity abstraction and message passing to model structured knowledge and unstructured language.
Automatic and human evaluations show the proposed model is more effective at achieving the goal and more human-like than baseline neural and rule-based models.
_summary.methodology: They construct an evolving knowledge graph over items, attributes, and entities that is updated with dialogue utterances; node embeddings are updated via message passing and entity abstraction, and an RNN encoder-decoder with attention and a copying mechanism generates utterances conditioned on node embeddings.
metrics: Task success (both agents select the shared item)
Human evaluation ratings: fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue systems / knowledge graph embeddings
